title: Possum - A Lay Of Newchumland
content:
آ آ آ آ So yer travï؟½linï؟½ for yer pleasure while yer writinï؟½ for the press?
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ yer huntinï؟½ arter ï؟½copyï؟½? well, Iï؟½ve heerï؟½d oï؟½ that. I guess
آ آ آ آ You are gorn ter write a story that is gorn ter be yer best,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Bout the ï؟½blunders anï؟½ advenchers ov a new chum in the west?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ you would be very thankful anï؟½ acknowledge any hint?
آ آ آ آ Well, I karnï؟½t say as I hankers fur ter see my name in print;
آ آ آ آ But I know a little story anï؟½ Iï؟½ll tell it out ov hand
آ آ آ آ If yerï؟½ll put it down in writinï؟½ that the swells kin understand
آ آ آ آ (Itï؟½s a story ov a new chum, and a story ov the land.)


آ آ آ آ He had lately kum from Ingland you cud tell it by ï؟½s cap
آ آ آ آ Fur ï؟½kerlonial experï؟½enceï؟½ (anï؟½ he got it, too, poor chap).
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas in town he met the squatter, anï؟½ he asked, as if in fun,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½If the boss ï؟½ud want a flunkey or a coachy on the run?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Well, it riz the bossï؟½s dander, anï؟½ he jumps clean orf ï؟½is ï؟½oss
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Now, me fresh, sweet-scented beauty, watyer givï؟½nus?ï؟½ sez the boss;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½I hev met yer kidney often, anï؟½ yer mighty fresh anï؟½ free,
آ آ آ آ But yer neednï؟½t think yer gorn ter come a-lardinï؟½ over me!ï؟½


آ آ آ آ But the new chum sed that ï؟½onest he was lookinï؟½ for a job,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ in spite of his appearance he had blued ï؟½is bottom bob.
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ as beggars karnï؟½t be choosers same as people wot are rich,
آ آ آ آ Said heï؟½d go as stooï؟½rd or gardï؟½ner, but he warnï؟½t partickler which.
آ آ آ آ Well, the joker seemed in earnest, so the boss began ter cool,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ he only blanked the new chum for a thundï؟½rinï؟½ jumpt-up fool.
آ آ آ آ Then he sed, ï؟½Well, thereï؟½s the fencinï؟½, if yerï؟½ll tramp it up from Perth,
آ آ آ آ The boys ï؟½ll find yer suï؟½thin pï؟½rï؟½aps, anï؟½ givï؟½ yer wat yer worth.ï؟½


آ آ آ آ Ov course the squatter never thort ter see ï؟½im any more,
آ آ آ آ But he waï؟½nï؟½t the kind ov new chum that the squatter tuk ï؟½im for;
آ آ آ آ No, he waï؟½nï؟½t the kind er cockeroach that onï؟½y kums ter shirk,
آ آ آ آ That wants ter git the sugar, but is friï؟½tened ov the work;
آ آ آ آ For he sold ï؟½is watch ï؟½nï؟½ joolï؟½ry, ï؟½nï؟½ lardi-dardy suits,
آ آ آ آ Stuck a swag upon his shoulder, ï؟½nï؟½ ï؟½is feet in blucher boots;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ I dunno how he did it, he was anythinï؟½ but strong,
آ آ آ آ But he ï؟½umped his bluey ninety mile anï؟½ kum to Bunglelong.

آ آ آ آ He earnt ï؟½is pound and tucker borinï؟½ holes anï؟½ runninï؟½ wire,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ heï؟½d work from dawn to sunset, anï؟½ he never seemed to tire;
آ آ آ آ But he must have suffered orful from the tucker anï؟½ the heat,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ the everlastinï؟½ trampinï؟½ made ï؟½im tender in the feet,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ he must hev thort ov England wï؟½en the everlastinï؟½ flies
آ آ آ آ Ware a-worrit, worrit, worrit, anï؟½ a-knawinï؟½ at ï؟½is eyes;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ he used to swear like thunder wï؟½en the yaller sergeant ants
آ آ آ آ Took a morninï؟½ stroll, promiscus, on the inside ov ï؟½is pants.

آ آ آ آ He uster make ï؟½is damper six or seven inches thick
آ آ آ آ It was doughey on the inside anï؟½ the shell was like a brick,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ while the damper made ï؟½im dream ov days ov long ago,
آ آ آ آ The little boodie rats ï؟½ud kum anï؟½ nibble out the dough.
آ آ آ آ He biled ï؟½is taters soggy, anï؟½ ï؟½is junk was biled to rags
آ آ آ آ (The little boodie rats ï؟½ud kum anï؟½ chew ï؟½s tucker bags),
آ آ آ آ But he took ï؟½is troubles cheerful, anï؟½ he fixed ï؟½em like a pome,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ writ ï؟½em in his darey to amuse the folks at home.

آ آ آ آ At first he flashed a coller anï؟½ was keerful with ï؟½is hat,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ heï؟½d black ï؟½is boots ov Sundays, but he soon grew out of that;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ he lernt ter bake ï؟½is damper, anï؟½ he leant to bile ï؟½is junk
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ sleep without a-getting up all night ter shake ï؟½is bunk.
آ آ آ آ He soon got out ov takinï؟½ ï؟½shorter cutsï؟½ across the flats,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ he learnt to fling ole bottles to the sorror of the rats,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ learnt to sling kerlonial and like the bushmanï؟½s way,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ it did us good to see ï؟½im smoke ï؟½is ï؟½niggerï؟½ in a clay.

آ آ آ آ He would sing anï؟½ play ï؟½is fiddle when we gathered round the blaze,
آ آ آ آ Till ole Frenchy got excited while heï؟½d play the Mascylays;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ Bill ï؟½ud take ï؟½is hat off while heï؟½d spout the Light Brigade,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ Scotchy got oneasy when the ï؟½Bony ï؟½Illsï؟½ was played.
آ آ آ آ So we got ter like the new chum for weï؟½d met with many wuss,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ we made it easy for ï؟½im anï؟½ he seemed to take to us:
آ آ آ آ The toilinï؟½ anï؟½ the trampinï؟½ was a-cookinï؟½ ï؟½im we found,
آ آ آ آ So we made ï؟½im cook anï؟½ stooï؟½rd just ter keep the chap around.

آ آ آ آ Well, the months went bakinï؟½ broilinï؟½ on until Christmas nexï؟½,
آ آ آ آ When we tramped it down to Perth to spend our ï؟½ollyday (and cheques);
آ آ آ آ But Possum sed heï؟½d save ï؟½is tin anï؟½ stay and mind the camp,
آ آ آ آ So we left ï؟½im in possession anï؟½ we started on our tramp;
آ آ آ آ (We useter call ï؟½im Possum, but for short we called ï؟½im Poss,
آ آ آ آ For ï؟½is eyes was black anï؟½ twinklinï؟½ and a little chap he was),
آ آ آ آ We never would have left ï؟½im if weï؟½d knowï؟½d (but thatï؟½s the rub),
آ آ آ آ Cominï؟½ back we found ï؟½im dyinï؟½ in ï؟½is gunyah in the scrub.

آ آ آ آ We fixed ï؟½im up anï؟½ nursed ï؟½im; but we seen without a doubt
آ آ آ آ That consumption was the matter, anï؟½ the chap was pegginï؟½ out;
آ آ آ آ But the lion heart inside ï؟½im was as strong anï؟½ stout as six,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ while heï؟½d smile anï؟½ thank us he would joke about ï؟½is fix;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ he said ï؟½twas very jolly to be dry-nursed in a tent,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ he reckoned that the Christmas was the best heï؟½d ever spent;
آ آ آ آ He would talk of ï؟½ome and Inglanï؟½ when ï؟½is head began ter swim,
آ آ آ آ But he never blamed the country that had been so ï؟½ard on him.

آ آ آ آ He would say, ï؟½I like the country; if a fellerï؟½s blind er halt,
آ آ آ آ Or if heï؟½s got konsumption, why it ainï؟½t the countryï؟½s fault.
آ آ آ آ The tea thatï؟½s boilï؟½d in billies is far sweeter stuff, I know,
آ آ آ آ Than the cursed drink wï؟½at blasted all my chances long ago.
آ آ آ آ I would hev cum out sooner if it was my destiny,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ I daresay that the country would have made a man ov me.
آ آ آ آ But wï؟½atï؟½s the good ov energy, anï؟½ watï؟½s the good er ï؟½pushï؟½
آ آ آ آ Wï؟½en a fellerï؟½s sick anï؟½ dyinï؟½ in a gunyah in the bush.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ But he tole me all about it as I sat beside ï؟½is bunk
آ آ آ آ How heï؟½d spent ï؟½is tin in Melbourne anï؟½ was allers gettinï؟½ drunk;
آ آ آ آ How he thort heï؟½d take it easy while he had a little gold,
آ آ آ آ And, before he turned the new leaf, how he scribbled on the old;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ among a lot ov nonsense wï؟½en ï؟½is mind began to drift,
آ آ آ آ He told me that the new leaf was a heavy leaf to lift.
آ آ آ آ But wï؟½ats the good er writinï؟½ this, itï؟½s nothinï؟½ very new,
آ آ آ آ The land will see enough ov it anï؟½ suffer for it, too.
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ he said wï؟½en he was dying, (when his lung was spit away)
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ we all was standinï؟½ round ï؟½im in the gunyah where he lay,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ he said, ï؟½Iï؟½ve watched the sunset when the wind began to ï؟½wooshï؟½,
آ آ آ آ Like a layer ov coals a-glowinï؟½ on the dark bed ov the bush;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ I felt my fingers slippinï؟½ slippinï؟½ slowly from the ropes,
آ آ آ آ Wen the West was cold like ashes like the ashes of my hopes;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ I Sit beside me Peter let me ï؟½old a bushmanï؟½s hand,
آ آ آ آ For Iï؟½m gorn to ï؟½ump my bluey through the gates ov Newchumland.ï؟½
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
